perturbation.explanation: Changing 'RECIPIENT' to 'CONTRACTOR' introduces ambiguity. While generally the terms 'RECIPIENT' and 'CONTRACTOR' are referring to the same entity that is receiving confidential information, by changing the naming convention, and not updating it across the whole text, it creates a situation where the rest of the document refers to 'RECIPIENT', leaving uncertain if the term 'CONTRACTOR' is indeed 'RECIPIENT'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: NON-DISCLOSURE AND INTELLECTUAL PROPER TY RIGHTS AGREEMENT This Agreement is between __________________(“OWNER”); and _______________________ an individual residing at ___________________________________________________ (“CONTRACTOR”). WHEREAS, OWNER has developed through substantia l effort, research, time, and expense certain inventions, design concepts, methodologies, technical know-how, copyrightable material and trade secrets directed and related to______________________________________________________ (“INFORMATION”); WHEREAS, OWNER desires to disclose the INFORM ATION on a confidential basis to RECIPIENT solely for the purposes of evaluating the INFORMATI ON for possible future business arrangements; and WHEREAS, OWNER wishes to maintain the confidentiality of the INFORMATION and the protection of OWNER'S intellectual property rights. NOW, THEREFORE, in consideration of the mutual promises, covenants, and conditions contained herein, the sufficiency of which is hereby acknowledged, the parties agree as follows: I. CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION A. OWNER agrees to disclose INFORMATION to RE CIPIENT to facilitate possible future business dealings between the parties. B. RECIPIENT agrees to receive such INFORMAT ION and to refrain from copying, disclosing, using, selling, or offering for sale any and all of said INFORMATION, other than at the request of OWNER, with the exceptions as provided in paragraph C herein. RECIPIENT agrees to keep confidential and refrain from disclosing any an d all of the INFORMATION, and to take all necessary and reasonable steps to prevent unauthorized disclosure or use of any and all of the INFORMATION. C. Notwithstanding paragraph B, RECIPIENT shall not be liable for disclosure or use of INFORMATION only if, and only to the extent that, said INFORMATION was in the public domain at the time it was disclosed by OWNER, or was known to and recorded in writing by RECIPIENT prior to the time of disclosure by OWNE R, or is received from a third party or passes into the public domain without breach of this Agreement. With respect to any INFORMATION known by RECIPIENT prior to the time of disclosure by OWNER that RECIPIENT believes to constitute the INFORMATION, or any portion thereof, RECIPIENT shall disclose to OWNER an adequate written description of the INFORMATION within fourteen (14) days of the disclosure by OWNER. D. This is not an offer for sale or license. No right or license is granted by OWNER to RECIPIENT in connection with the technical information or inventions disclosed under this agreement. All documents or materials constituting the INFORMATION and all reproductions thereof shall at all times remain the sole property of OWNER an d shall promptly be returned by RECIPIENT upon request. E. This Agreement shall remain in force in spite of disclosure of the INFORMATION by OWNER in the form of patent applications, copyright a pplications, or other disclosures by OWNER.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'RECIPIENT' with 'CONTRACTOR' in the agreement definition. The contradicted text then refers extensively to 'RECIPIENT' regarding non-disclosure obligations. This creates a strong ambiguity and potential contradiction because it's unclear if the obligations assigned to 'RECIPIENT' also apply to the entity now defined as 'CONTRACTOR', thus creating misalignment of terminology. This ambiguity makes the agreement unenforceable because the 'RECIPIENT' is not clearly defined.

perturbation.original_text: OWNER agrees to disclose INFORMATION to RE CIPIENT to facilitate possible future business dealings between the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: OWNER agrees to disclose INFORMATION to the USER to facilitate possible future business dealings between the parties.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'RECIPIENT' to 'USER' creates confusion. The agreement initially defines 'RECIPIENT' in section 1, now 'USER' is undefined, but has to fulfill all the requirements stated by the 'RECIPIENT' role, since all obligations, limitations and rights should apply, as if both terms are interchangeable. It becomes uncertain whether the obligations outlined for 'RECIPIENT' also apply to the 'USER', or if 'USER' is a separate entity.

RECIPIENT agrees to receive such INFORMAT ION and to refrain from copying, disclosing, using, selling, or offering for sale any and all of said INFORMATION, other than at the request of OWNER, with the exceptions as provided in paragraph C herein. RECIPIENT agrees to keep confidential and refrain from disclosing any an d all of the INFORMATION, and to take all necessary and reasonable steps to prevent unauthorized disclosure or use of any and all of the INFORMATION. C. Notwithstanding paragraph B, RECIPIENT shall not be liable for disclosure or use of INFORMATION only if, and only to the extent that, said INFORMATION was in the public domain at the time it was disclosed by OWNER, or was known to and recorded in writing by RECIPIENT prior to the time of disclosure by OWNE R, or is received from a third party or passes into the public domain without breach of this Agreement. perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'RECIPIENT' who has obligations to maintain confidentiality of the information. The changed text replaces 'RECIPIENT' with 'USER'. The contradicted text uses both 'USER' and 'RECIPIENT', outlining obligations for 'RECIPIENT'. Because 'USER' is not defined, and the change replaces 'RECIPIENT' with 'USER', it's unclear whether 'USER' has the same obligations as 'RECIPIENT', creating a strong in-text contradiction according to the definition of Misaligned Terminology in the prompt. 'USER' is undefined, but has to fulfill all the requirements stated by the 'RECIPIENT' role, since all obligations, limitations and rights should apply, as if both terms are interchangeable. It becomes uncertain whether the obligations outlined for 'RECIPIENT' also apply to the 'USER', or if 'USER' is a separate entity.

perturbation.original_text: RECIPIENT agrees to receive such INFORMAT ION and to refrain from copying, disclosing, using, selling, or offering for sale any and all of said INFORMATION, other than at the request of OWNER, with the exceptions as provided in paragraph C herein.
perturbation.changed_text: CONTRACTOR agrees to receive such INFORMAT ION and to refrain from copying, disclosing, using, selling, or offering for sale any and all of said INFORMATION, other than at the request of OWNER, with the exceptions as provided in paragraph C herein.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'RECIPIENT' to 'CONTRACTOR' in section I.B, while leaving other sections referring to 'RECIPIENT', creates an in-text contradiction, by leading to uncertainty as to which entity is bound by the confidentiality obligations. This in-text contradiction weakens the enforceability of the NDA since it becomes unclear who is held responsible for maintaining confidentiality.

perturbation.justification: The original text uses 'RECIPIENT' which is consistent with the 'Contradicted Text' which also refers to 'RECIPIENT'. The changed text replaces 'RECIPIENT' with 'CONTRACTOR' in section I.B. However, the 'Contradicted Text' contains multiple references to both 'CONTRACTOR' and 'RECIPIENT'. The change introduces an in-text contradiction because the 'Contradicted Text' now has two different terms referring to potentially the same entity ('CONTRACTOR' and 'RECIPIENT'), but the changed text does not clarify if these two terms are interchangeable. This creates ambiguity and uncertainty, fulfilling the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction'. The ambiguity as to who is bound creates a strong contradiction, by leading to uncertainty as to which entity is bound by the confidentiality obligations.

perturbation.original_text: RECIPIENT agrees to keep confidential and refrain from disclosing any an d all of the INFORMATION, and to take all necessary and reasonable steps to prevent unauthorized disclosure or use of any and all of the INFORMATION.
perturbation.changed_text: THE PARTY agrees to keep confidential and refrain from disclosing any an d all of the INFORMATION, and to take all necessary and reasonable steps to prevent unauthorized disclosure or use of any and all of the INFORMATION.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'RECIPIENT' to 'THE PARTY' creates confusion. Is THE PARTY, the RECIPIENT, the OWNER? It is creating an in-text contradiction by making it unclear which entity is obligated to maintain confidentiality. This ambiguity weakens the legal enforceability of the NDA, as it becomes challenging to establish who is bound by the confidentiality clause.
perturbation.contradicted_text: CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION A. OWNER agrees to disclose INFORMATION to RE CIPIENT to facilitate possible future business dealings between the parties. B. RECIPIENT agrees to receive such INFORMAT ION and to refrain from copying, disclosing, using, selling, or offering for sale any and all of said INFORMATION, other than at the request of OWNER, with the exceptions as provided in paragraph C herein. THE PARTY agrees to keep confidential and refrain from disclosing any an d all of the INFORMATION, and to take all necessary and reasonable steps to prevent unauthorized disclosure or use of any and all of the INFORMATION. C. Notwithstanding paragraph B, RECIPIENT shall not be liable for disclosure or use of INFORMATION only if, and only to the extent that, said INFORMATION was in the public domain at the time it was disclosed by OWNER, or was known to and recorded in writing by RECIPIENT prior to the time of disclosure by OWNE R, or is received from a third party or passes into the public domain without breach of this Agreement. With respect to any INFORMATION known by RECIPIENT prior to the time of disclosure by OWNER that RECIPIENT believes to constitute the INFORMATION, or any portion thereof, RECIPIENT shall disclose to OWNER an adequate written description of the INFORMATION within fourteen (14) days of the disclosure by OWNER. All documents or materials constituting the INFORMATION and all reproductions thereof shall at all times remain the sole property of OWNER an d shall promptly be returned by RECIPIENT upon request. E. This Agreement shall remain in force in spite of disclosure of the INFORMATION by OWNER in the form of patent applications, copyright a pplications, or other disclosures by OWNER.
perturbation.justification: The original text clearly defines the 'RECIPIENT' as the party bound by confidentiality. The changed text replaces 'RECIPIENT' with 'THE PARTY'. The contradicted text refers to both 'OWNER', 'RECIPIENT', and now 'THE PARTY'. The introduction of 'THE PARTY' without clear definition creates ambiguity as to who is obligated to maintain confidentiality, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction and uncertainty about the obligations and rights of each party as per the definition of Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding paragraph B, RECIPIENT shall not be liable for disclosure or use of INFORMATION only if, and only to the extent that, said INFORMATION was in the public domain at the time it was disclosed by OWNER, or was known to and recorded in writing by RECIPIENT prior to the time of disclosure by OWNE R, or is received from a third party or passes into the public domain without breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding paragraph B, PERSONNEL shall not be liable for disclosure or use of INFORMATION only if, and only to the extent that, said INFORMATION was in the public domain at the time it was disclosed by OWNER, or was known to and recorded in writing by RECIPIENT prior to the time of disclosure by OWNE R, or is received from a third party or passes into the public domain without breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'RECIPIENT' to 'PERSONNEL' creates confusion as to whether 'PERSONNEL' refers to employees of the 'RECIPIENT', agents, or a completely unrelated third party. This introduces an in-text contradiction, as the 'RECIPIENT' remains defined for other clauses. This ambiguity weakens the liability exception, since it is unclear who is not liable for information disclosure: 'PERSONNEL' or 'RECIPIENT'.

RECIPIENT agrees to receive such INFORMAT ION and to refrain from copying, disclosing, using, selling, or offering for sale any and all of said INFORMATION, other than at the request of OWNER, with the exceptions as provided in paragraph C herein. Notwithstanding paragraph B, PERSONNEL shall not be liable for disclosure or use of INFORMATION only if, and only to the extent that, said INFORMATION was in the public domain at the time it was disclosed by OWNER, or was known to and recorded in writing by RECIPIENT prior to the time of disclosure by OWNE R, or is received from a third party or passes into the public domain without breach of this Agreement. perturbation.justification: The change from 'RECIPIENT' to 'PERSONNEL' in paragraph C of the Contradicted Text creates an ambiguity because the document defines 'RECIPIENT' in section A and uses it in section B. By replacing 'RECIPIENT' with 'PERSONNEL' in section C, it's unclear whether the liability exception applies to 'RECIPIENT' or 'PERSONNEL', introducing a Misaligned Terminology In-Text Contradiction, according to definition 3. This directly impacts the enforceability of the agreement, as it's uncertain who is liable for information disclosure.

perturbation.original_text: All documents or materials constituting the INFORMATION and all reproductions thereof shall at all times remain the sole property of OWNER an d shall promptly be returned by RECIPIENT upon request.
perturbation.changed_text: All documents or materials constituting the INFORMATION and all reproductions thereof shall at all times remain the sole property of OWNER an d shall promptly be returned by THE COMPANY upon request.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'RECIPIENT' to 'THE COMPANY' creates confusion. It introduces an in-text contradiction because the agreement is between the OWNER and RECIPIENT, not the OWNER and THE COMPANY. 'THE COMPANY' is not a defined term, nor is it clear whether it is referring to the OWNER or to a third party related to the RECIPIENT.
